title: The media estimator, closed loop — delay-based AIMD on iroh path-RTT (bandwidth ramp)
design_ref: realtime-media-over-iroh.md C1 solution point 2 (the media estimator MUST be authoritative); TC-CC2 bandwidth-ramp
harness: relay-loadtest roq-send --adaptive (src/roq.rs): baseline RTT = running min; if path-RTT > base+rtt_budget_ms (50) -> bitrate *=0.7 (floor min-kbps 8); else += 8 kbps up to target. Recomputes datagram payload size per frame.
method: 64 kbps source, node-1->node-2, start UNSHAPED (healthy ~2ms baseline), drop a 40 kbit netem cap at t=8s mid-call. Compare fixed vs adaptive.

ramp-adaptive.bitrate_response: 64 -> 14 -> 8 kbps within ~1s of the cap dropping
ramp-adaptive.verdict: bounded: estimator backs off in <1s; RTT stabilizes; receiver gets a continuous degraded-but-live stream

finding: The C1 estimator the design requires not only CAN read iroh's path-RTT — it ACTS on it effectively. On a mid-call bandwidth drop, fixed bitrate bufferbloats unbounded (RTT 7s+ and climbing, call dead); delay-based AIMD backs off in under a second and bounds RTT to ~1s while delivering a continuous lossless (degraded) stream. Peak RTT cut 4.4x (7024->1609ms), mean 3.6x (2446->689ms). Open question #1 from the round summary is closed: the RTT signal is actionable, and adaptation is the difference between a dead call and a degraded-but-live one.
caveats: Residual RTT ~1s (not ~50ms): the initial overshoot before backoff seeds a queue, and the netem rate qdisc + relay-path contention hold a standing queue even at 8 kbps. A real engine would pace down faster and/or flush; this is a GCC/BBR-lite stand-in.

The cap-from-START case (e10-adaptive.sh / e10both.sh) does NOT bound RTT: if the path is already saturated when the call begins, the baseline locks high and the standing queue never drains regardless of source rate or send-buffer size. Adaptation helps the DEGRADATION case (TC-CC2), not the join-an-already-dead-link case.
Synthetic CBR; a raw-UDP side-baseline (to separate iroh/relay contribution from the netem qdisc) remains the E10 follow-on.
